coredata.dc:title: Simultaneous humidity and temperature measurement sensor based on two cascaded long-period fiber gratings
coredata.dc:creator: Han, Zhang
Chao, Jiang
Xiping, Zhu
Pei, Wang
Tingshui, Cao
Changning, Liu
Simei, Sun
coredata.dc:description:
An optical fiber sensor based on two cascaded long-period fiber gratings (LPFG) for simultaneous measurement of humidity and temperature is proposed. In the cascade structure, the resonant peak wavelengths of the two LPFGs (i.e. LPFG1 and LPFG2) are in different bands, in which the resonant peak wavelengths of LPFG1 are between 1325 nm and 1465 nm, and the resonant peak wavelengths of LPFG2 are between 1470 nm and 1575 nm. Then, the humidity sensitive material and thermal sensitive material are combined with LPFG1 and LPFG2 respectively to achieve high-sensitivity humidity and temperature measurement. In our sensor structure, the mixture of humidity sensitive material polyimide (PI) and graphene quantum dots (GQDs) was coated on the surface of LPFG1, and the humidity sensitivity of 78 pm/%RH was obtained in the range of 45%RH ∼ 75%RH. Then, the surface of LPFG2 was encapsulated with dimethyl silicone oil (DSO) thermal sensitive material, and the temperature sensitivity of LPFG2 reached 445 pm/
∘
C. Finally, the sensitivity measurement matrix is constructed by using the temperature and humidity sensitivities obtained by two LPFG, which can realize the simultaneous measurement of humidity and temperature and eliminate the cross-sensitivity. The sensor has the advantages of simple structure, good stability, low cost, high sensitivity and double parameter measurement, and it has a certain practical application prospect.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Optical fiber sensor
Long period fiber grating
Dimethyl silicone oil
Polyimide
Graphene quantum dots
Temperature and relatively humidity

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articlenumber articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table body mmlmath acknowledge affil articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor highlightsabst orcid primabst ref

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Simultaneous measurement of RH and temperature
Influence of axial strain on temperature measurement

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The work was supported in part by the Fund of Graduate Innovation Research of Hubei Normal University, China ( 20220498 ), the Middle-aged and Young Outstanding Innovation Team of Hubei Normal University, China ( T201502 , 2019CZ09 ), and the Middle-aged and Young Science and Technology Innovation Team of Hubei Province University, China ( T2020014 ). We would also like to thank Dr. B. Xu from China Jiliang University for theoretical calculation and simulation.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic of two LPFGs cascaded sensor.
Transmission spectra of bare LPFG1(a) and LPFG2 (b) in air at room temperature.
Transmission spectra of (a) LPFG1 coated with humidity sensitive materials and (b) LPFG2 surrounded by thermal sensitive material.
(a) Micrograph and (b) SEM of LPFG1 coated with PI+GQD.
Transmission spectrum of LPFG1 and LPFG2 cascaded.
Temperature and humidity of experimental setup of the sensor.
Shifts and linear fittings of Dip 1 of LFPG1 with RH changing.
(a) Shifts and (b) linear fittings of Dip 2 of LPFG2 with RH changing.
(a) Shifts and (b) linear fittings of Dip 3 of LPFG1 coated PI with RH increasing.
Three experimental results of Dip 1 of LFPG1 measuring RH.
Response time of Dip 1 of the sensor measuring RH.
(a) Shifts and (b) linear fittings of Dip 1 of LPFG1 with temperature change.
(a) Shifts and (b) linear fitting of Dip 1 of Dip 2 of LPFG2 with temperature change.
Repeatability of the sensor measuring temperature.
Linear fittings of the central wavelengths of (a) Dip 1 and (b) Dip 2 with axial strain change.
Comparison of LPFG humidity sensors with different humidity materials.
Comparison of the humidity and temperature simultaneous measurement sensors.

head.ce:title: Simultaneous humidity and temperature measurement sensor based on two cascaded long-period fiber gratings

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Physics and Electronic Science, Hubei Normal University, Huangshi, Hubei, 435002, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Physics and Electronic Science, Hubei Normal University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Physics and Electronic Science, Hubei Normal University, Huangshi, Hubei, 435002, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para:
An optical fiber sensor based on two cascaded long-period fiber gratings (LPFG) for simultaneous measurement of humidity and temperature is proposed. In the cascade structure, the resonant peak wavelengths of the two LPFGs (i.e. LPFG1 and LPFG2) are in different bands, in which the resonant peak wavelengths of LPFG1 are between 1325 nm and 1465 nm, and the resonant peak wavelengths of LPFG2 are between 1470 nm and 1575 nm. Then, the humidity sensitive material and thermal sensitive material are combined with LPFG1 and LPFG2 respectively to achieve high-sensitivity humidity and temperature measurement. In our sensor structure, the mixture of humidity sensitive material polyimide (PI) and graphene quantum dots (GQDs) was coated on the surface of LPFG1, and the humidity sensitivity of 78 pm/%RH was obtained in the range of 45%RH ∼ 75%RH. Then, the surface of LPFG2 was encapsulated with dimethyl silicone oil (DSO) thermal sensitive material, and the temperature sensitivity of LPFG2 reached 445 pm/
∘
C. Finally, the sensitivity measurement matrix is constructed by using the temperature and humidity sensitivities obtained by two LPFG, which can realize the simultaneous measurement of humidity and temperature and eliminate the cross-sensitivity. The sensor has the advantages of simple structure, good stability, low cost, high sensitivity and double parameter measurement, and it has a certain practical application prospect.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Relative humidity (RH) and temperature are two basic physical quantities. Their simultaneous monitoring is particularly important in practical applications such as food processing, biochemical engineering, environmental monitoring and pharmaceutical industry. Compared with other types of temperature and humidity sensors, optical fiber temperature and humidity sensors have the advantages of compact structure, high sensitivity, strong robustness, stable performance, corrosion resistance and anti-electromagnetic interference [1].

Optical fiber sensors for measuring temperature and humidity at the same time often have four typical structures. The first structure is composed of all fiber Mach Zehnder interferometer (MZI) or Fabry Perot interferometer (FPI) combining with humidity sensitive materials. In Ref. [1–3], MZI is combined with different humidity sensitive materials realizing simultaneous measurement of humidity and temperature. In Ref. [4], compact MZI and FPI and humidity sensitive materials are used to measure humidity and temperature simultaneously. In Ref. [5–10], FPI is combined with different humidity sensitive materials realizing simultaneous measurement of humidity and temperature. The second structure is composed of optical fiber interferometers and optical fiber Bragg grating (FBG) cascading. In Ref. [11], FBG cascaded with MZI coated with humidity sensitive materials for simultaneous measurement of temperature and humidity. In Ref. [12–14], FPI with humidity sensitive materials cascaded FBG for simultaneous measurement of humidity and temperature. However, in these sensor structures, some sensors have complex structures and difficult manufacturing, and some sensors have low sensitivity.

The third structure for simultaneous measurement of humidity and temperature is composed of optical fiber grating and wet sensitive materials. In references [15,16], long-period fiber grating (LPFG) coated with humidity sensitive material realizes the simultaneous measurement of humidity and temperature. In Ref. [17–19], fiber Bragg grating (FBG) coated with humidity sensitive material realizes the simultaneous measurement of humidity and temperature. The fourth structure for simultaneous measurement of humidity and temperature is composed of LPFG and FBG cascaded. In reference [20,21], LPFG coated with wet sensitive materials cascaded FBG achieving simultaneous measurement of humidity and temperature. However, the humidity sensitivity of sensors strongly depends on the performance of wet sensitive materials, and the temperature sensitivities of sensors are often low, which is not conducive to temperature measurement. In recent years, some methods have been used to improve the temperature and humidity sensitivity of optical fiber grating sensors. For example, coating polyurethane acrylate [22] or PDMS [23] on the surface of LPFG has obtained higher temperature sensitivity. Coating more sensitive wet sensitive materials such as graphene oxide, PEG/PVA or gelatin on the surface of LPFG have also obtained higher humidity sensitivity [24–27].

In addition to the above four common types, there are many other structures that can also be used for simultaneous measurement of humidity and temperature, such as optical fiber surface plasmon resonance and wet sensitive material [28], U-shaped optical fibers and humidity sensitive material [29], microfiber coupler and humidity sensitive material [30]. These structures can usually obtain the high sensitivity of humidity and temperature, but the manufacturing process is relative complex.

In the current structure, there is no report that humidity sensitive material and thermal sensitive material are coated on the surface of two cascaded LPFGs respectively for simultaneous measurement of humidity and temperature. In the paper, two LPFGs cascaded for measuring simultaneously humidity and temperature is proposed. In LPFG1, polyimide and graphene quantum dots (PI+GQDs) were coated on the surface of LPFG1, and the high sensitivity of humidity is obtained. In LPFG2, the surface of LPFG2 is encapsulated by dimethyl silicone oil (DSO), which greatly improves the temperature sensitivity. The experimental results show that the humidity sensitivity of the sensor reaches 78 pm/%RH and the temperature sensitivity reaches 445 pm/
∘
C. In addition, utilizing the sensitivity matrix of two LPFGs, the simultaneous measurement of humidity and temperature can be realized, and the cross sensitivity between them can be eliminated. This kind of two parameter simultaneous measurement LPFG sensor is expected to be applied in more fields.

The schematic of the sensor structure is shown in Fig. 1. The sensor consists of two cascaded LPFGs (LPFG1 and LPFG2). The resonant peak wavelengths of two LPFGs in different bands is used to measure RH and temperature, respectively. PI+GQDs are coated on the surface of LPFG1 for RH measurement. LPFG2 is packaged with DSO for temperature measurement. The sensitivity of two LPFGs is used to construct the measurement matrix, which realizes the simultaneous measurement of RH and temperature, and eliminates the cross sensitivity between them.

The manufacturing process of the sensor is briefly described as follows. First, two LPFG1 and LPFG2 are written on one SMF using a high-frequency CO2 laser point by point method at an interval of 5 cm. The periods of two LPFGs are
520
μ m
and
630
μ m
respectively. The lengths of two gratings are about 20 mm. SMF is a standard ordinary single-mode fiber produced by Wuhan YOFC Ltd. The main parameters of the CO2 laser are the output power of 2.5 W, and the wavelength of
10 . 6
μ m
, and the frequency of 20 kHz, and the inscription speed of 10 mm/s. Finally, the LPFG is put into a 300 °C furnace for annealing for 2 h for standby.

In LPFG, the resonant wavelength (
λ
m
) of the loss band is given as follows
(1)
λ
m
=
(
n
e f f
c o r e
−
n
e f f
c l a d , m
)
Λ
where
n
e f f
c o r e
and
n
e f f
c l a d , m
are the effective refractive indices (RI) of the core mode and the m th order cladding mode respectively. Λ is the grating period of LPFG.

The transmission spectra of two bare LPFGs in air at room temperature are shown in Fig. 2. It can be seen from Fig. 2 that the resonance peak wavelengths of the two LPFGs are in different bands, in which LPFG1 has three resonance peaks in the band of 1325 nm to 1465 nm, and LPFG2 has two resonance peaks in the band of 1470 nm to 1570 nm. Using COMSOL software to simulate the two LPFGs, it can be concluded that the three resonance dips in the transmission spectrum of LPFG1 are caused by the coupling of core mode LP01 and cladding mode LP02 (resonance wavelength 1337 nm), LP03 (resonance wavelength 1380 nm) and LP06 (resonance wavelength 1462 nm), respectively. The two resonance dips of LPFG2 transmission spectrum are caused by the coupling of core mode LP01 and cladding mode LP02 (resonance wavelength 1472 nm) and LP04 (resonance wavelength 1542 nm), respectively.

PI is an organic polymer material with good comprehensive properties, which has good temperature stability, acid resistance, radiation resistance, non-toxicity and biocompatibility and so on. In addition, PI has the characteristic of linear hygroscopic expansion, and it can be used as an excellent humidity sensitive material for humidity sensors. There have been many research reports on the humidity sensor composed of PI and optical fiber [13,17]. However, the water absorption capacity of PI material limits the further improvement of humidity sensor sensitivity. In order to realize the function of high precision and wide range of humidity sensor, it is necessary to find new humidity sensitive materials.

Graphene quantum dots (GQDs), as a new type of nano-graphene sheet, have attractive physical/chemical properties. The electron density on GQDs decreases when GQDs adsorbs water particles, and resulting in the decrease of refractive index (RI). At the same time, the GQDs layer will expand after interacting with water molecules. Therefore, GQDs has been used in electrochemical humidity sensors. However, due to its nano-scale and water solubility, GQDs cannot be well combined with optical fiber, which limits the application of GQDs in optical fiber sensors. However, the hydroxyl, carboxyl and carbonyl groups on the surface of GQDs make it easy to combine with polymers. Zhao et al. mixed GQDs and polymer PVA and coated them on the surface of the optical fiber interferometer to prepare two humidity sensors. It was found that the sensitivity of the sensor was further improved after adding GQDs to PVA [5,31].

Therefore, we mixed GQDs and PI at a ratio of 1:50 for the first time and coated them on the surface of LPFG1 to achieve high-sensitivity humidity measurement. Two LPFG1 with the same parameters were written using the above method. After their annealing, they were immersed in 3-aminopropyltriethoxysilane (3-apts) solution for 5 min to increase the binding force between the fiber grating surface and the PI solution. Then, PI was coated on the surface of the first LPFG1 and PI + GQD was coated on the surface of the second LPFG1. The two gratings were dried in a drying oven at 180 °C for 2 h. The spectrum of LPFG1 coated with PI is shown by the black curve in Fig. 3(a), and Dip 3 is selected to measure humidity. The spectrum of LPFG1 coated with PI + GQD is shown by the red curve in Fig. 3(a). Compared with the spectrum of bare LPFG1 (the blue curve in Fig. 3(a)), it was found that the spectral intensity of LPFG1 coated wet sensitive materials slightly increased, and the resonance peak shifted slightly to the right.

DSO has good chemical/physical stability, high thermal expansion coefficient and thermo-optical coefficient. As an excellent thermal sensitive material, DSO is used to improve the temperature sensitivity of sensor. The process of packaging DSO on the surface of LPFG2 is as follows: First, LPFG2 is inserted into a glass tube with an inner diameter of 1 mm, and then the glass tube is immersed in a box containing DSO. DSO is slowly sucked into the glass tube through capillary effect until the whole glass tube is filled with DSO. Then, AB glue is used to seal both ends of the glass tube to prevent DSO leakage. Finally, DSO was encapsulated on the surface of LPFG2. The red curve in Fig. 3(b) shows the spectrum of LPFG2 packaged by DSO. When LPFG2 is encapsulated in DSO, the resonant peak wavelengths shift slightly to the right.

Fig. 4 shows the micrograph and SEM of LPFG1 coated with PI + GQDs. In Fig. 4(a), it can be seen that the thickness of PI + GQDs film is about
4 . 4
μ m
. In Fig. 4(b), it can be found that there are many GQDs particles in PI glue, and the size is in the order of nanometers. Fig. 5 shows the spectrum of two LPFGs cascaded together. It can be seen from Fig. 5 that the spectra of the two LPFGs are separated in the total spectrum. The three resonant wavelengths on the left side of the spectrum are generated by LPFG1, and the three resonant wavelengths on the right side of the spectrum are generated by LPFG2. According to the theoretical analysis results of LPFG, the resonance peak of long wavelength length is usually excited by the higher-order cladding mode, and the resonance peak excited by the higher-order cladding mode usually has higher sensitivity [32]. In the resonance peaks of LPFG1, Dip 1 is excited by LP06 (the highest-order cladding mode), and in the resonance peaks of LPFG2, Dip 2 is excited by LP04 (the highest-order cladding mode). Therefore, we select Dip 1 and Dip 2 respectively to study the sensing characteristics of the sensor.

For LPFG1, the surface of LPFG1 is coated with moisture-sensitive materials PI+GDQs. According to Eq. (1), when the ambient humidity changes, the hygroscopic expansion or dehumidification contraction of the humidity sensitive material on the LPFG1 surface will change the period of LPFG1 and the effective RI of the cladding, thus leading to the shift of the resonance peak wavelength of LPFG1. The humidity is measured by demodulating the resonance peak wavelength shifts of LPFG1. Therefore, the RH sensitivity of LPFG1 is calculated as
(2)
S
R H
=
d
λ
m
d R H
=
λ
m
(
d Λ
Λ d R H
+
d Δ
n
e f f
Δ
n
e f f
d R H
)
Here
d Λ
Λ d R H
is the expansion coefficient of the moisture-sensitive material PI+GQDs, and
d Δ
n
e f f
Δ
n
e f f
d R H
is the effective RI change coefficient of moisture-sensitive material PI+GQDs. Since two coefficients of humidity sensitive materials are highly dependent on the composition and structure of the materials, it is generally difficult to obtain specific data, so they are not quantitatively calculated here. However, the two coefficients of PI+GQDs are generally large, so the RH sensitivity of LPFG1 is high.

For LPFG2, the surface of LPFG2 is encapsulated with thermal sensitive material DSO. When the ambient temperature varies, DSO will undergo thermal expansion and RI change, resulting in the resonant wavelength shift of LPFG2. The temperature is measured by monitoring the wavelength shift of the resonance peak of LPFG2. The temperature sensitivity of LPFG2 can be solved by the following formula:
(3)
S
T
=
d
λ
m
d T
=
λ
m
(
d Λ
Λ d T
+
d Δ
n
e f f
Δ
n
e f f
d T
)
Where
d Λ
Λ d T
is the sum of the thermal expansion coefficients of DSO and silica, and
d Δ
n
e f f
Δ
n
e f f
d T
is the sum of the thermo-optical coefficients of DSO and silica. Because two coefficients of DSO are large, the LPFG2 has high sensitivity of temperature.

Fig. 6 is the temperature and humidity experimental setup of the sensor. In Fig. 6, they mainly include a low-polarization broadband light source (BBS, FL-ASE, Beijing Conquer) with ∼ 400 nm bandwidth (1250–1650 nm), an optical spectrum analyzer (OSA, Yokogawa, AQ6370D) with the resolution of 0.02 nm, a fiber optic circulator, a sensing head and a programmable temperature and humidity test chamber (WHTH-80L, Dongguan, China) etc. The humidity and temperature characteristics of the sensor can be studied with a programmable temperature and humidity test box. In the RH experiments, the test box is constant at 25 °C, and the RH varies from 45%RH to 75%RH with a step of 5%RH. At each humidity measurement point, keep it for 10 min and record the data after the humidity is stable. In the temperature experiments, the test box is constant at 40%RH, and the temperature varies from 35 °C to 90 °C with a step of 5 °C. At each temperature measurement point, keep it for 10 min and record the data after the temperature is stable.

Firstly, we measure the RH responses of the sensor by using the experimental setup of Fig. 6. Fig. 7 shows the shifts and linear fittings of Dip 1 of LPFG1 with RH changing. As can be seen from Fig. 7, Dip 1 shows a large red shift with the increase of RH. When the RH increases, the RH sensitivity of Dip 1 is about 78 pm/%RH. When the RH decreases, the RH sensitivity of Dip 1 is about 95 pm/%RH. The experimental results show that there is a certain difference in the sensitivity of the sensor when the humidity rises and falls, indicating that the sensor has a certain hysteresis error. The shifts and linear fittings of Dip 2 of LPFG2 with RH changing are shown in Fig. 8. It can be found from Fig. 8 that the Dip 2 is barely shifts with RH changing, and the RH sensitivity of LPFG2 is approximately zero. Because LPFG2 is not coated with humidity sensitive material, LPFG2 is insensitive to RH.

Then, we also carried out humidity experiments on LPFG1 coated with PI film to verify the effect of adding GQDs to PI. Fig. 9 displays the shifts and linear fittings of Dip 3 of LPFG1 coated with PI with RH increasing. It can be seen from Fig. 9 that the RH sensitivity of Dip 3 is 66 pm/%RH within the same humidity measurement range. Comparing the experimental data obtained, it was found that the RH sensitivity of LPFG1 increased from 66 pm/%RH to 78 pm/%RH after adding GQDs to PI solution, and the RH sensitivity increased by about 18%.

The humidity experiment of the sensor is carried out every other month to test the repeatability of the sensor. Fig. 10 shows the results of three RH measurement experiments with increase of RH under the same conditions. It can be seen that the measured data of Dip 1 in the three experiments are basically the same, indicating that the sensor has good repeatability and reliability. By fitting the average values of the three experimental data, the RH sensitivity is 76 pm/%RH, and the linear fitting coefficient is 0.993. It is basically consistent with the result of single measurement (78 pm/%RH), and the measurement error is only 2.5%.

Finally, the response time and recovery time of the sensor for measuring humidity are also studied. First, the test chamber is set to a stable environment of 70%RH and 25 °C. Then the sensor is moved quickly from the laboratory environment (25 °C and 40%RH) to the test chamber through the small window of test chamber. At the same time, OSA automatically collects and records the spectrum of sensor every 10 s until the spectrum is completely stable. The sensor is kept for a period of time in this stable state, and then it is moved quickly to the laboratory environment (25 °C and 40%RH) through the small window. At the same time, OSA automatically collects and records the spectrum of sensor every 10 s until the spectrum is completely stable. The spectral data are plotted in Fig. 11. As can be seen from Fig. 11, the response time and recovery time of the sensor are about 64.3 s and 56.8 s respectively. The small response time indicates that the sensor responds quickly to the change of humidity. The response times of the sensor are closely related to the water absorption capacity and film thickness of PI+GQDs film.

The following is a simple comparison between our LPFG humidity sensor and several existing LPFG humidity sensors in terms of sensitivity, dynamic range and response time. The results are shown in Table 1. Compared with other humidity sensors, our humidity sensor is basically equivalent to these sensors sensitivity and dynamic range in Refs. [15,21,27], but less than the sensitivity in Refs. [16,25,26]. Since most LPFG humidity sensors do not discuss the response time, it is impossible to compare, but the response time of our sensors is still relatively short. In addition, after our sensor is combined with thermal sensitive materials, it can also realize the simultaneous measurement of humidity and temperature.

We measure the temperature responses of the sensor by using the test chamber of Fig. 6. Fig. 12 shows the shifts and linear fittings of Dip 1 of LPFG1 with temperature change. As can be seen from Fig. 12, Dip 1 shows a large red shift with the increase of temperature. The temperature sensitivity of Dip 1 is 202 pm/
∘
C as the temperature increases, and the temperature sensitivity of Dip 1 is 163 pm/
∘
C as the temperature decreases. The experimental results show that LPFG1 coated with PI+GQDs has certain the sensitivity of temperature, mainly because PI and GQDs have also certain thermal sensitivity.

Fig. 13 shows the shifts and linear fittings of Dip 2 of LPFG2 with temperature change. It can be seen from Fig. 13 that as the temperature increases, Dip 2 shows a larger red-shift. The temperature sensitivity of Dip 2 is 445 pm/
∘
C as the temperature increases from 35 °C to 60 °C, and the temperature sensitivity of Dip 2 is 93 pm/
∘
C when the temperature increases from 60 °C to 90 °C. The temperature sensitivity of Dip 2 is 99 pm/
∘
C when the temperature decreases from 90 °C to 60 °C, and the temperature sensitivity of Dip 2 is 383 pm/
∘
C when the temperature decreases from 60 °C to 35 °C. The experimental results show that DSO packaged LPFG2 has high temperature sensitivity, mainly because DSO has high thermal expansion coefficient and thermos-optical coefficient. In addition, the sensitivity in the temperature range of 35 °C to 60 °C is 4.8 times that in the temperature range of 60 °C to 90 °C.

We conduct a temperature experiment on the sensor every other month to test the repeatability of the sensor. Since both LPFG1 and LPFG2 are sensitive to temperature, Fig. 14 records the results of three experiments of Dip 1 and Dip 2 with the increase of temperature under the same conditions. It can be seen that the data of Dip 1 and Dip 2 measured in the three experiments are basically the same, indicating that the sensor has good repeatability.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Simultaneous measurement of RH and temperature
ce:section.ce:para:
The RH and temperature sensitivities of Dip 1 and Dip 2 is used to construct the following sensitivity matrix:
(4)
Δ
λ
D i p 1
Δ
λ
D i p 2
=
α
R H 1
β
T 1
α
R H 2
β
T 2
Δ R H
Δ T
where
Δ
λ
D i p 1
is the wavelength shift of Dip 1, and
Δ
λ
D i p 2
is the wavelength shift of Dip 2.
α
R H 1
is the RH sensitivity of Dip 1, and
α
R H 2
is the RH sensitivity of Dip 2.
β
T 1
is the temperature sensitivity of Dip 1, and
β
T 2
is the temperature sensitivity of Dip 2.

According to the sensitivity data measured in the experiment, the following two sensitivity measurement matrices can be obtained:
(5)
Δ R H
Δ T
=
0 . 078
0 . 202
0
0 . 445
− 1
Δ
λ
D i p 1
Δ
λ
D i p 2
,
( 35
° C < T < 60
° C )
(6)
Δ R H
Δ T
=
0 . 078
0 . 202
0
0 . 093
− 1
Δ
λ
D i p 1
Δ
λ
D i p 2
,
( 60
° C < T < 90
° C )
Taking use of matrices (5) and (6), the ambient humidity and temperature can be measured simultaneously to eliminate cross-sensitivity.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Influence of axial strain on temperature measurement
ce:section.ce:para:
When the sensor is applied in practice, the influence of axial strain change on temperature measurement may also be considered. In order to eliminate the influence of axial strain on the sensor, we carried out axial strain experiments on the sensor. Fig. 15(a) shows the linear fitting of the central wavelength of Dip 1 with axial strain increasing. As can be seen from Fig. 15(a), the axial strain sensitivity of Dip 1 is 1.4 pm/
μ ɛ
as the axial strain increases. Fig. 15(b) displays the linear fitting of the central wavelength of Dip 2 with axial strain increasing. It can be seen that the axial strain sensitivity of Dip 2 is 3.0 pm/
μ ɛ
as the axial strain increases.

According to the similar matrix method, the following two sensitivity measurement matrices can be obtained to eliminate axial strain cross-sensitivity while measuring temperature.
(7)
Δ T
Δ ɛ
=
0 . 202
1 . 4
0 . 445
3 . 0
− 1
Δ
λ
D i p 1
Δ
λ
D i p 2
,
( 35
° C < T < 60
° C )
(8)
Δ T
Δ ɛ
=
0 . 202
1 . 4
0 . 093
3 . 0
− 1
Δ
λ
D i p 1
Δ
λ
D i p 2
,
( 60
° C < T < 90
° C )
Taking use of matrices (7) and (8), the ambient temperature and axial strain can also be measured simultaneously to eliminate cross-sensitivity.

The performance of our sensor is compared with that of the optical fiber sensor for simultaneous measurement of humidity and temperature. The results are shown in Table 2. It can be seen from Table 2 that the temperature sensitivity of most sensors is not very high, which affects the temperature measurement. Although the sensitivity of our sensor is not the best, its humidity sensitivity is higher than some of the sensors in the Refs. [2,5,13,15,17,20], and lower than those in the Refs. [1,3,4,11,12,21,28–30]. Our temperature sensitivity is also higher than that of most sensors in Table 2 and only lower than that in Refs. [28,29]. High sensitivity means that the sensor has potential for practical application. In addition, compared with the configuration of some sensors in Table 2, the sensor structure proposed by us is simple and reliable.

In this study, an optical fiber sensor based on two LPFGs cascaded for the simultaneous measurement of RH and temperature is proposed and experimentally verified. The resonant peak wavelengths of two LPFGs are in different bands respectively. LPFG1 with resonant peak wavelength at low band is combined with humidity sensitive materials, which is mainly used to measure RH. The experimental result shows that the RH sensitivity reaches 78 pm/%RH, and the RH measurement has good linearity and repeatability. LPFG2 with resonant peak wavelengths at high band is combined with thermal sensitive materials to achieve high sensitivity temperature measurement. The experimental result shows that the temperature sensitivity is up to 445 pm/
∘
C, and the temperature measurement also has good linearity and repeatability. Utilizing the RH and temperature sensitivities obtained from the experiments, the sensitivity measurement matrix is constructed, which realizes the simultaneous measurement of RH and temperature and eliminates the cross-sensitivity.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The work was supported in part by the Fund of Graduate Innovation Research of Hubei Normal University, China (20220498), the Middle-aged and Young Outstanding Innovation Team of Hubei Normal University, China (T201502, 2019CZ09), and the Middle-aged and Young Science and Technology Innovation Team of Hubei Province University, China (T2020014). We would also like to thank Dr. B. Xu from China Jiliang University for theoretical calculation and simulation.

Opt. Commun.
